extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property requires that setting dizziness=true (all other inputs unchanged) never lowers acuity. Rule r3 has priority 100 (far above every other rule's max priority of 3) and fires only when all 11 listed inputs that it names are true, INCLUDING dizziness; it sets acuity := self_care (the lowest acuity). This creates a violation.

Compare the two inputs that differ only in dizziness, with all other 10 symptom inputs in r3's conjunction set to true and vision_loss=false:

BEFORE (dizziness=false): r3 cannot fire (it requires dizziness=true). Firing rules include r1/r6/r8/r9 (severe_headache, abdominal_pain, syncope, focal_weakness), each priority 3 -> emergent, plus several priority-2 urgent rules. Highest priority among firing rules is 3 -> acuity = emergent.

AFTER (dizziness=true, everything else identical): Now r3's full conjunction (chest_pain, abdominal_pain, dyspnea, severe_headache, vomiting, syncope, rash, focal_weakness, leg_swelling, dizziness) is satisfied. r3 has priority 100, which beats all the priority-3 emergent rules. So r3 wins -> acuity = self_care.

self_care is the lowest acuity in the order [self_care, routine, urgent, emergent], while the before-value was emergent (the highest). Setting dizziness true thus drops acuity from emergent to self_care, directly violating the property. The witness_assignment gives the AFTER state (dizziness=true) that yields self_care; the corresponding BEFORE state (same assignment but dizziness=false) yields emergent.
